text: The gallbladder is sutured to the abdominal wall, freeing one of the arms. The duodenum is visible, sometimes buried in fat. The surgeon begins dissection about three to four centimeters below the antrum, entering a plane between the stomach and proximal transverse colon. The transverse colon is grasped and retracted, with the right gastroepiploic artery visible on the right side of the screen. Successful procedure requires good traction and counter traction.

The surgeon retracts the colon to the patient's right lower quadrant and mobilizes it, revealing the second part of the duodenum. The duodenum is grasped with a Cartier grasper and retracted to the patient's left, facilitating the cocharization process.

The surgeon applies traction and counter traction on the second part of the duodenum, using blunt dissection techniques. Sutures are placed on the duodenum to assist with the upcoming duodenotomy and falciform flap creation. TilePro technology is utilized to confirm the tumor's location, which is situated just above the center.

The surgeon opens the duodenal wall between the twelve and nine o'clock positions, revealing the minor papilla and the tumor. A 5-0 PDS suture is used to provide traction, allowing for the removal of the polyp with a negative margin.

The surgeon removes the tumor while ensuring the location of the major and minor papillae. The mucosal defect is closed, with some bleeding observed at the mucosal edges due to the use of cold scissors. The bleeding typically ceases once the defect is sutured closed.

The surgeon identifies the sphincter of Oddi and prepares to perform a sphincteroplasty. A finale catheter is placed through the cystic duct and passed down to the duodenum, where it is grasped to aid in identifying the sphincter. The sphincter is sutured to the mucosa using a 5-0 absorbable suture.
The surgeon closes the defect posteriorly and proceeds to close the lateral duodenotomy in one layer using canal stitches. Silk sutures are then used to secure the falciform flap.
